title: Inflation-Adjusted Stock Returns: Real S&P 500 Returns 19...
description: Think the stock market always goes up? See how inflation secretly devours S&P 500 gains—and why the 1970s is a warning for today's investors.

schemas.headline: Inflation-Adjusted Stock Returns: Real S&P 500 Returns 1928–2026
schemas.description: Think the stock market always goes up? See how inflation secretly devours S&P 500 gains—and why the 1970s is a warning for today's investors.

mainEntity.name: What are inflation adjusted stock market returns?

acceptedAnswer.text: Inflation-adjusted stock market returns (also known as real returns) measure the actual purchasing power gained from your investments after subtracting the inflation rate. If the S&P 500 returns 10% in a year, but inflation is 3%, your real return is roughly 7%.

mainEntity.name: What are the real S&P 500 returns historically?

acceptedAnswer.text: Historically, the nominal average return of the S&P 500 has been around 10% annually. However, after adjusting for long-term inflation averages of roughly 3%, the real S&P 500 returns are closer to 6% to 7% per year since 1928.

mainEntity.name: How do you calculate stock market returns after inflation?

acceptedAnswer.text: To calculate stock market returns after inflation accurately, use the formula: Real Return = [(1 + Nominal Return) / (1 + Inflation Rate)] - 1. For simple estimations, you can just subtract the inflation rate from the nominal return, though it is slightly less precise.

mainEntity.name: How did inflation affect stock returns in the 1970s?

acceptedAnswer.text: The 1970s is a classic example of inflation destroying wealth. While nominal stock returns appeared positive for the decade, inflation averaged over 7% per year. As a result, the real purchasing power of investors actually declined, leading to negative real returns for the decade.

mainEntity.name: Why do real returns matter for retirement planning?

acceptedAnswer.text: Real returns are crucial for retirement planning because your future expenses will cost more due to inflation. If you base your retirement goals solely on nominal returns, you might reach your dollar target but find that the money buys significantly less than you expected.
